Question: A 65-kg person jumps from the first floor window of a burning building and lands almost vertically on the ground with a horizontal velocity of 3 m/s and vertical velocity of $$-9 \text{m/s}$$ . Upon impact with the ground he is brought to rest in a short time. The force experienced by his feet depends on whether he keeps his knees stiff or bends them. Find the force on his feet in each case. A. First find the impulse on the person from the impact on the ground. Calculate both its magnitude and direction. B. Find the average force on the feet if the person keeps his leg stiff and straight and his center of mass drops by only 1 cm vertically and 1 cm horizontally during the impact. C. Find the average force on the feet if the person bends his legs throughout the impact so that his center of mass drops by 50 cm vertically and 5 cm horizontally during the impact. D. Compare the results of part (b) and (c), and draw conclusions about which way is better. You will need to find the time the impact lasts by making reasonable assumptions about the acceleration opposite to the motion. Although the force is not constant during the impact, working with constant average force for this problem is acceptable.
o3 Answer:
Known data  
Mass: m = 65 kg   
Initial velocity just before the feet touch the ground  

               v⃗ i = ( 3.0 m s⁻¹ ) î  + ( – 9.0 m s⁻¹ ) ĵ  
  (speed |v⃗ i| = √(3²+9²) = √90 = 9.49 m s⁻¹)

After the impact the person is at rest (v⃗ f = 0).

The only external forces during the very short impact are the force of the ground on the feet F⃗ G and the weight m g  (downward).  We shall work with constant average forces even though the real forces are not constant.

A.  Impulse delivered by the ground  

Impulse on the person :   J⃗ = m ( v⃗ f – v⃗ i ) = – m v⃗ i

        J⃗ = – 65 kg ( 3 î – 9 ĵ ) m s⁻¹
            = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) N·s

Magnitude  
        |J⃗| = √(195² + 585²) = 195 √10 ≈ 6.16 × 10² N·s

Direction  
• Upward (positive y) and backward (negative x).  
• Angle with the vertical:  θ = tan⁻¹(|Jx|/|Jy|) = tan⁻¹(1/3) ≈ 18.4° away from the vertical.

B.  Stiff-leg landing (centre of mass moves only 1 cm down and 1 cm horizontally)

Total stopping distance along the original direction  

        s₁ = √(0.010² + 0.010²) m = 0.0141 m

Assume a constant deceleration a₁ opposite to the motion:

        |a₁| = v² /(2 s₁) = (9.49)² /(2·0.0141) ≈ 3.18 × 10³ m s⁻²

Stopping time  

        t₁ = v / |a₁| = 9.49 / 3.18×10³ ≈ 2.98 × 10⁻³ s  (≈ 3 ms)

Average net force on the person  

        F⃗ avg = J⃗ / t₁
                = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) / 0.00298
                = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.96×10⁵ ĵ ) N

Magnitude of the net force      |F⃗ avg| ≈ 2.07 × 10⁵ N  
Ground–on-feet force (add the weight)  

        F⃗ ground = F⃗ avg  +  m g (–ĵ)
                   = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.96×10⁵ ĵ )  –  (0 î – 637 ĵ)
                   = ( – 6.55×10⁴ î + 1.97×10⁵ ĵ ) N

        |F⃗ ground| ≈ 2.08 × 10⁵ N   (≈ 3.3 ×10² times the body weight!)

C.  Landing with bent knees  
(The centre of mass drops 50 cm vertically and 5 cm horizontally)

Stopping distance  

        s₂ = √(0.50² + 0.05²) m = 0.502 m

Constant deceleration magnitude  

        |a₂| = v² /(2 s₂) = 9.49² /(2·0.502) ≈ 8.96 × 10¹ m s⁻²

Stopping time  

        t₂ = v / |a₂| = 9.49 / 89.6 ≈ 1.06 × 10⁻¹ s  (≈ 0.11 s)

Average net force on the person  

        F⃗ avg = J⃗ / t₂
                = ( – 195 î + 585 ĵ ) / 0.106
                = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 5.52×10³ ĵ ) N

Magnitude of the net force      |F⃗ avg| ≈ 5.8 × 10³ N  

Ground–on-feet force (add weight)

        F⃗ ground = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 5.52×10³ ĵ )  –  (0 î – 637 ĵ)
                   = ( – 1.84×10³ î + 6.16×10³ ĵ ) N

        |F⃗ ground| ≈ 6.4 × 10³ N   (≈ 9.7 times the body weight)

D.  Comparison and conclusion  

• Stiff–leg landing:  ≈ 2 × 10⁵ N  (≈ 320 g)  
• Bent–leg landing:   ≈ 6 × 10³ N  (≈ 10 g)

Bending the knees increases the stopping distance and the stopping time by almost two orders of magnitude, which reduces the average force on the feet by the same factor.  The enormous force in the stiff-leg case is far above the tolerance of bones and joints and would almost certainly cause serious injury, while the bent-leg force is within what the human body can safely withstand.  

Therefore, when landing from a jump (or in any similar situation) it is far safer to bend the knees and allow the body to decelerate over the largest possible distance and time.

Here is one way to organize the solution.

(Throughout we take the person’s mass m=65 kg, his arrival velocity  
v⃗ = 3 î − 9 ĵ (m/s), and his final velocity after impact 0.)

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
A) Impulse from the ground  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

By definition the impulse on the person is  
 J⃗ = Δp⃗ = m (v⃗_final − v⃗_initial)  
Since v⃗_final=0 we get  
 J⃗ = − m v⃗ = −65 (3 î − 9 ĵ)  
 J⃗ = −195 î + 585 ĵ (Units: N·s)  

Magnitude:  
 |J⃗| = √[ (−195)² + 585² ] ≃ √[38 025 + 342 225] = √380 250 ≃ 616.7 N·s  

Direction:  
 tan θ = J_y / J_x = 585 / (−195) = −3.  
In the usual +x‐to‐+y CCW convention this lies in quadrant II, so θ ≃ 180° − 71.6° = 108.4° measured from +x.  
You can also say “the impulse points mostly upward (ĵ) and slightly backward (−î).”

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
B) Average force, “stiff legs” (1 cm drop & 1 cm slide)  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

We must estimate how long the deceleration lasts.  If during the impact  
the COM moves only Δy=1 cm=0.01 m upward (to absorb the −9 m/s) and Δx=1 cm=0.01 m backward (to absorb the +3 m/s), then under constant‐deceleration kinematics one finds two different decelerations in x and in y—but the shorter of the two stoppage times will dominate the net impulse.

Better (and simpler) is to note that the vertical deceleration is the more severe limiter:  
 a_y = −v_y² / (2 Δy) = −(9 m/s)² / (2·0.01 m) = −81 / 0.02 = −4050 m/s².  
Hence the vertical‐stop time is  
 t ≃ Δt_y = |v_y| / |a_y| = 9 / 4050 ≃ 0.00222 s.  

Since the total impulse magnitude is 617 N·s, an average net force (impulse ÷ time) is  
 F_net,avg ≃ 617 N·s / 0.00222 s ≃ 2.78×10^5 N.  

If you like, you can add the person’s weight mg≈65·9.8=637 N, but over ~2 ms that is negligible.  
Thus  
 Average impact force ≃ 3×10^5 N (≈300 kN)  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
C) Average force, “bent legs” (50 cm drop & 5 cm slide)  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

Now Δy=0.50 m, Δx=0.05 m.  Again the vertical deceleration will give the shortest time:  
 a_y = −v_y² / (2 Δy) = −81 / (2·0.50) = −81 / 1.0 = −81 m/s²  
so  
 Δt ≃ |v_y| / |a_y| = 9 / 81 = 0.111 s.  

Hence  
 F_net,avg ≃ J / Δt = 617 N·s / 0.111 s ≃ 5560 N.  

Even if you add mg ≃ 637 N, you get F_feet ≃ 6200 N.  

So roughly  
 Average impact force ≃ 6×10^3 N (≈6 kN)  

––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  
D) Comparison & conclusion  
––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––  

• Stiff-leg stop (Δy=1 cm): F_avg ≃ 3×10^5 N  
• Bent-leg stop (Δy=50 cm): F_avg ≃   6×10^3 N  

By bending your knees you increase the stopping distance by a factor of ~50, so the average force on your feet (and hence on your skeleton) is reduced by about the same factor.  In practice that is the single most important thing you can do to reduce impact forces and avoid injury.

**Given Information:**
*   Mass of the person, $m = 65 \text{ kg}$
*   Initial velocity just before impact, $\vec{v}_i = (3.0 \text{ m/s})\hat{i} - (9.0 \text{ m/s})\hat{j}$
*   Final velocity after impact, $\vec{v}_f = \vec{0}$

First, let's calculate the person's weight and change in kinetic energy, which will be useful in later parts.
*   Weight, $\vec{W} = m\vec{g} = (65 \text{ kg})( -9.8 \text{ m/s}^2 \hat{j}) = -637 \text{ N} \hat{j}$
*   Change in kinetic energy, $\Delta K = K_f - K_i = 0 - \frac{1}{2}m v_i^2$
    $v_i^2 = v_{ix}^2 + v_{iy}^2 = (3.0 \text{ m/s})^2 + (-9.0 \text{ m/s})^2 = 9.0 + 81.0 = 90.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$
    $\Delta K = -\frac{1}{2}(65 \text{ kg})(90.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2) = -2925 \text{ J}$

---

### A. Impulse on the Person

The impulse ($\vec{J}$) on the person is equal to the change in his momentum ($\Delta\vec{p}$).

1.  **Calculate the change in momentum:**
    $\vec{J} = \Delta\vec{p} = m\vec{v}_f - m\vec{v}_i = m(\vec{v}_f - \vec{v}_i)$
    $\vec{J} = 65 \text{ kg} \left( (0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j}) - (3.0\hat{i} - 9.0\hat{j}) \text{ m/s} \right)$
    $\vec{J} = 65 \text{ kg} (-3.0\hat{i} + 9.0\hat{j}) \text{ m/s}$
    $\vec{J} = (-195\hat{i} + 585\hat{j}) \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}$

2.  **Calculate the magnitude of the impulse:**
    $|\vec{J}| = \sqrt{J_x^2 + J_y^2} = \sqrt{(-195)^2 + (585)^2}$
    $|\vec{J}| = \sqrt{38025 + 342225} = \sqrt{380250} \approx 617 \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}$
    Rounding to two significant figures, $|\vec{J}| \approx 6.2 \times 10^2 \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}$.

3.  **Calculate the direction of the impulse:**
    The direction is given by the angle $\theta$ measured counterclockwise from the positive x-axis.
    $\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{J_y}{J_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{585}{-195}\right) = \arctan(-3)$
    Since the x-component is negative and the y-component is positive, the vector is in the second quadrant.
    $\theta = 180^\circ - \arctan(3) = 180^\circ - 71.6^\circ = 108.4^\circ$
    Rounding to two significant figures, $\theta \approx 110^\circ$.

**Answer for A:** The impulse on the person is $\vec{J} = (-195\hat{i} + 585\hat{j}) \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}$. Its magnitude is approximately **$6.2 \times 10^2 \text{ N} \cdot \text{s}$** and its direction is **$110^\circ$** counterclockwise from the positive x-axis.

---

### B. Average Force with Stiff Legs

**Given:**
*   Displacement of center of mass, $\vec{d} = (1.0 \text{ cm})\hat{i} - (1.0 \text{ cm})\hat{j} = (0.010 \text{ m})\hat{i} - (0.010 \text{ m})\hat{j}$

To find the average force, we first need to find the duration of the impact, $\Delta t$. We can find $\Delta t$ by relating the work done by the net force to the change in kinetic energy.
$W_{net} = \Delta K$
The work done by the average net force is $W_{net} = \vec{F}_{net, avg} \cdot \vec{d}$.
The average net force is related to impulse by $\vec{F}_{net, avg} = \vec{J} / \Delta t$.
Combining these, we get:
$(\vec{J} / \Delta t) \cdot \vec{d} = \Delta K$
$\Delta t = \frac{\vec{J} \cdot \vec{d}}{\Delta K}$

1.  **Calculate the impact time, $\Delta t$:**
    $\vec{J} \cdot \vec{d} = (-195)(0.010) + (585)(-0.010) = -1.95 - 5.85 = -7.8 \text{ N} \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{m}$
    $\Delta t = \frac{-7.8 \text{ J} \cdot \text{s}}{-2925 \text{ J}} \approx 0.00267 \text{ s}$

2.  **Calculate the average net force, $\vec{F}_{net, avg}$:**
    $\vec{F}_{net, avg} = \frac{\vec{J}}{\Delta t} = \frac{-195\hat{i} + 585\hat{j}}{0.00267} \approx (-7.31 \times 10^4 \hat{i} + 2.20 \times 10^5 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$

3.  **Calculate the average force on the feet from the ground, $\vec{F}_{ground, avg}$:**
    The net force is the sum of the ground force and gravity: $\vec{F}_{net, avg} = \vec{F}_{ground, avg} + \vec{W}$.
    $\vec{F}_{ground, avg} = \vec{F}_{net, avg} - \vec{W}$
    $\vec{F}_{ground, avg} = (-7.31 \times 10^4 \hat{i} + 2.20 \times 10^5 \hat{j}) \text{ N} - (-637 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$
    $\vec{F}_{ground, avg} \approx (-7.31 \times 10^4 \hat{i} + 2.21 \times 10^5 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$

4.  **Calculate the magnitude of the ground force:**
    $|\vec{F}_{ground, avg}| = \sqrt{(-7.31 \times 10^4)^2 + (2.21 \times 10^5)^2} \approx 2.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$

**Answer for B:** The average force on the feet is approximately **$2.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$**.

---

### C. Average Force with Bent Legs

**Given:**
*   Displacement of center of mass, $\vec{d}' = (5.0 \text{ cm})\hat{i} - (50 \text{ cm})\hat{j} = (0.050 \text{ m})\hat{i} - (0.50 \text{ m})\hat{j}$

The impulse and change in kinetic energy are the same as before. We follow the same procedure as in Part B.

1.  **Calculate the new impact time, $\Delta t'$:**
    $\Delta t' = \frac{\vec{J} \cdot \vec{d}'}{\Delta K}$
    $\vec{J} \cdot \vec{d}' = (-195)(0.050) + (585)(-0.50) = -9.75 - 292.5 = -302.25 \text{ N} \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{m}$
    $\Delta t' = \frac{-302.25 \text{ J} \cdot \text{s}}{-2925 \text{ J}} \approx 0.103 \text{ s}$

2.  **Calculate the new average net force, $\vec{F'}_{net, avg}$:**
    $\vec{F'}_{net, avg} = \frac{\vec{J}}{\Delta t'} = \frac{-195\hat{i} + 585\hat{j}}{0.103} \approx (-1.89 \times 10^3 \hat{i} + 5.68 \times 10^3 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$

3.  **Calculate the new average force on the feet, $\vec{F'}_{ground, avg}$:**
    $\vec{F'}_{ground, avg} = \vec{F'}_{net, avg} - \vec{W}$
    $\vec{F'}_{ground, avg} = (-1.89 \times 10^3 \hat{i} + 5.68 \times 10^3 \hat{j}) \text{ N} - (-637 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$
    $\vec{F'}_{ground, avg} \approx (-1.89 \times 10^3 \hat{i} + 6.32 \times 10^3 \hat{j}) \text{ N}$

4.  **Calculate the magnitude of the ground force:**
    $|\vec{F'}_{ground, avg}| = \sqrt{(-1.89 \times 10^3)^2 + (6.32 \times 10^3)^2} \approx 6.6 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Answer for C:** The average force on the feet is approximately **$6.6 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$**.

---

### D. Comparison and Conclusion

*   **Force with stiff legs (Part B):** $2.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$
*   **Force with bent legs (Part C):** $6.6 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Comparison:**
The force experienced by the person's feet when landing with stiff legs is significantly greater than when landing with bent legs. The ratio of the forces is:
$\frac{\text{Force (stiff)}}{\text{Force (bent)}} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N}}{6.6 \times 10^3 \text{ N}} \approx 35$
The force is about 35 times larger when the knees are kept stiff.

**Conclusion:**
It is much better and safer to land with bent knees. Bending the knees increases the distance over which the body's center of mass comes to a stop. This, in turn, increases the duration of the impact ($\Delta t$). According to the impulse-momentum theorem, the average force is given by $\vec{F}_{avg} = \Delta\vec{p} / \Delta t$. Since the change in momentum ($\Delta\vec{p}$) is the same for both landing styles, increasing the time ($\Delta t$) over which the momentum change occurs drastically reduces the average force experienced. The much smaller force experienced when bending the knees greatly reduces the risk of serious injury, such as broken bones.

Question: Two identical objects (such as billiard balls) have a one-dimensional collision in which one is initially motionless. After the collision, the moving object is stationary and the other moves with the same speed as the other originally had. Show that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
Answer: Conservation of momentum demands $$m_{1} v_{\text{1,i}} + m_{2} v_{\text{2,i}} = m_{1} v_{\text{1,f}} + m_{2} v_{\text{2,f}}$$ . We are given that $$m_{1} = m_{2}$$ , $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{2,f}}$$ , and $$v_{\text{2,i}} = v_{\text{1,f}} = 0$$ . Combining these equations with the equation given by conservation of momentum gives $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{1,i}}$$ , which is true, so conservation of momentum is satisfied. Conservation of energy demands $$\frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{\text{1,i}}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{\text{2,i}}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{\text{1,f}}^{2} + \frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{\text{2,f}}^{2}$$ . Again combining this equation with the conditions given above give $$v_{\text{1,i}} = v_{\text{1,i}}$$ , so conservation of energy is satisfied.

### 1. Define the System and Variables

First, we need to define the state of the system *before* and *after* the collision.

*   Let the two identical objects be Object 1 and Object 2.
*   Since they are identical, they have the same mass, which we will call **m**.
*   Let the initial velocity of Object 1 be **v**.

*   The final velocity of Object 1 is **0** (it becomes stationary).
*   The final velocity of Object 2 is **v** (it moves with the same speed the other originally had).

*   Initial velocity of Object 1: $v_{1i} = v$
*   Mass of Object 2: $m_2 = m$
*   Initial velocity of Object 2: $v_{2i} = 0$

**After Collision:**
*   Final velocity of Object 1: $v_{1f} = 0$
*   Final velocity of Object 2: $v_{2f} = v$

---

### 2. Show that Momentum is Conserved

The principle of conservation of momentum states that the total momentum before the collision must equal the total momentum after the collision.

**Formula for momentum:** $p = \text{mass} \times \text{velocity}$

**A. Calculate the total initial momentum ($P_{initial}$):**
The total initial momentum is the sum of the momentum of both objects before the collision.

$P_{initial} = (m_1 \times v_{1i}) + (m_2 \times v_{2i})$
$P_{initial} = (m \times v) + (m \times 0)$
$P_{initial} = mv$

**B. Calculate the total final momentum ($P_{final}$):**
The total final momentum is the sum of the momentum of both objects after the collision.

$P_{final} = (m_1 \times v_{1f}) + (m_2 \times v_{2f})$
$P_{final} = (m \times 0) + (m \times v)$
$P_{final} = mv$

**C. Compare:**
Since $P_{initial} = mv$ and $P_{final} = mv$, we have:
$P_{initial} = P_{final}$

**Conclusion: Momentum is conserved.**

---

### 3. Show that Kinetic Energy is Conserved

The principle of conservation of kinetic energy states that the total kinetic energy before the collision must equal the total kinetic energy after the collision. (This is the definition of a perfectly elastic collision).

**Formula for kinetic energy:** $KE = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{mass} \times \text{velocity}^2$

The total initial kinetic energy is the sum of the kinetic energy of both objects before the collision.

$KE_{initial} = (\frac{1}{2}m_1v_{1i}^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m_2v_{2i}^2)$
$KE_{initial} = (\frac{1}{2}mv^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m(0)^2)$
$KE_{initial} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$

**B. Calculate the total final kinetic energy ($KE_{final}$):**
The total final kinetic energy is the sum of the kinetic energy of both objects after the collision.

$KE_{final} = (\frac{1}{2}m_1v_{1f}^2) + (\frac{1}{2}m_2v_{2f}^2)$
$KE_{final} = (\frac{1}{2}m(0)^2) + (\frac{1}{2}mv^2)$
$KE_{final} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$

**C. Compare:**
Since $KE_{initial} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ and $KE_{final} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$, we have:
$KE_{initial} = KE_{final}$

**Conclusion: Kinetic energy is conserved.**

By calculating the total momentum and total kinetic energy both before and after the collision, we have shown that:

1.  The total momentum of the system remains **mv**.
2.  The total kinetic energy of the system remains **$\frac{1}{2}mv^2$**.

Because both quantities are unchanged by the collision, we have successfully demonstrated that both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved for this specific scenario. This is a classic example of a **perfectly elastic one-dimensional collision** between two objects of equal mass.
